evidence_quotes.quote: The issue: Anthem is an online game.
an increase in prices compared to the best past conditions.
There is no purchase anymore, there are only hidden rentals behind obscure vocabulary.
combined with forcing consumers to comply with arbitrary rules

paragraphs.text: First of all, thank you for this opportunity of expression. A few years ago I bought a game online: Anthem. The game was not exceptional (it was even rather missed), but it remained sympathetic to play, especially as a multi-player. The issue: Anthem is an online game. It needs to be connected to servers owned by the studio (BioWare) or editor (EA). Thus, when EA decided to cut the servers, the game became inaccessible.I paid 60 to access the game. I have discussed several times with others who would have liked to return to play with friends on the occasion. Now it is impossible. We were warned that the game would be inaccessible if EA decided to cut off access. Is it legal? Yes. But is it honest? I do not think so. I have paid for a game, and I find myself without anything. And beyond my person, the game is now inaccessible to everyone. When a cultural element disappears, it is always a loss. This is combined with forcing consumers to comply with arbitrary rules, always on our dependants. It is the publisher or shop that decides on our access, it is they who still decide on our right to play, it is they who decide to withdraw the game and, in case of disagreement, which prohibit consumer unions and force them to compete in the countries or states that arrange them. But it is always the consumer who pays the bill, whether it is money or loss. I buy a product. That I am not the holder of the exploitation rights is one thing, but I should not be afraid to no longer access this product if the shop refuses it. Today I have several games that I love, but I don’t know whether I will be able to pass them on to future generations, because almost all of them depend on updates, on connecting to servers, and on the desire of a corporation whose primary goal is to maximise their profit, even if it depends on those who finance them. Today, there is no benefit for the consumer to go through paperless. Connectivity only brings the risk of losing its purchase, the risk of losing a favourite work, the risk of falling into an online casino (FIFA and other gatchas), of losing as a game for what they sell more. We have lost timeless access to a purchase (CD) for access conditioned by one or more arbitrary entities (internet), all with an increase in prices compared to the best past conditions. Consumer rights are largely lost. There is no purchase anymore, there are only hidden rentals behind obscure vocabulary. There are solutions but you know how it works: these solutions will only be put in place under pressure. Love yourself, give us the right to play; the right to find our works, our characters, our decoration, our feelings and our pleasure to play. Thank you for your attention!
